subject: Physics
q: The positive feedback is required by an amplifier to act as an oscillator. The feedback here means
question: The positive feedback is required by an amplifier to act as an oscillator. The feedback here means
options: External input is necessary to sustain ac signal in output.
A portion of the output power is returned back to the input.
Feedback can be achieved by LR network.
The base-collector junction must be forward biased.
solution: When the amplifier connects with positive feedback, it acts as the oscillator. The feedback employed here is positive feedback, this means some amount of the output voltage is given to the input. Hence, the circuit continues its process without any input to the oscillator. The positive feedback is also known as regenerative feedback because the system sustains or regenerates itself without any input source. Hence, the oscillator is nothing but an amplifier with the positive feedback.

subject: Physics
q: A zener diode with 5 V zener voltage is used to regulate an unregulated dc voltage input of 25 V. For a $400 \Omega$ resistor connected in series, the zener current is found to be 4 times load current. The load current $\left(I_L\right)$ and load resistance $\left(R_{\mathrm{L}}\right)$ are :
question: A zener diode with 5 V zener voltage is used to regulate an unregulated dc voltage input of 25 V. For a $400 \Omega$ resistor connected in series, the zener current is found to be 4 times load current. The load current $\left(I_L\right)$ and load resistance $\left(R_{\mathrm{L}}\right)$ are :
solution:
From the circuit diagram,
$\begin{aligned}
& 5 \mathrm{i}=\frac{20}{400}=\frac{1}{20} \mathrm{~A} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{i}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~A}=10 \mathrm{~mA}=\text { Load current }
\end{aligned}$
Also, $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=5 \mathrm{~V}$
$\therefore \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{5}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \Omega=500 \Omega$

q: For using a multimeter to identify diode from electrical components, choose the correct statement out of the following about the diode
question: For using a multimeter to identify diode from electrical components, choose the correct statement out of the following about the diode
options: It is two terminal device which conducts current in both directions.
It is two terminal device which conducts current in one direction only
It does not conduct current gives an initial deflection which decays to zero.
It is three terminal device which conducts current in one direction only between central terminal and either of the remaining two terminals
solution: A diode is a two-terminal device that conducts current in forward bias and does not conduct current in reverse bias i.e. it conducts current in one direction only.

q: The I-V characteristics of a p-n junction diode in forward bias is shown in the figure. The ratio of dynamic resistance, corresponding to forward bias voltage of 2 V and 4 V respective is
question: The I-V characteristics of a p-n junction diode in forward bias is shown in the figure. The ratio of dynamic resistance, corresponding to forward bias voltage of 2 V and 4 V respective is
solution:
Dynamic resistance of a diode is defined as, R=∆V∆i.

At forward bias voltage of 2 V,

R1=∆V∆i=2.1-210-5×10-3=20 Ω

At forward bias voltage of 4 V,

R2=∆V∆i=4.2-4250-200×10-3=4 Ω.

Therefore,

R1R2=204=51

q: In an n-p-n common emitter (CE) transistor the collector current changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> for the change in base current from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, respectively. The current gain of transistor is _____.
question: In an n-p-n common emitter (CE) transistor the collector current changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> for the change in base current from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, respectively. The current gain of transistor is _____.
solution:
The change in collector current for the given transistor can be written as

∆Ic=16 mA-5 mA=11 mA

The change in base current for the given transistor can be written as

∆Ib=200 μA-100 μA= 100 μA

The formula to calculate the current gain in CE configuration is given by

β=∆Ic∆Ib   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required quantity.

β=∆Ic∆Ib=11 mA100 μA=110

subject: Physics
q: The forward biased diode connection is :
question: The forward biased diode connection is :
solution:
A junction diode is said to be forward-biased when the positive terminal of the external battery is connected to the p-region and the negative terminal to the n-region of the diode.

The potential of p-side is more positive that of n-side, hence diode is in forward biasing. In forward biasing it acts as short circuit, hence current flows.

In case of forward biased circuit P-junction should be at higher potential.

subject: Physics
q: A potential barrier of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> exists across a p-n junction. An electron enters the junction from the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>-side with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The speed with which electron enters the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.: (Given mass of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge on electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.)
question: A potential barrier of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> exists across a p-n junction. An electron enters the junction from the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>-side with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The speed with which electron enters the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.: (Given mass of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge on electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.)
solution:
Applying conservation of energy

-eVn+12mvi2=-eVp+12mvf2

We know, Vn-Vp=potential barrier=0.4 V

Then, we have, -eVn-Vp+12mvi2=12mvf2

⇒-1.6×10-19×0.4+12×9×10-31×36×1010=12×9×10-31 vf2

⇒-0.64×10-19+1.62×10-19=12×9×10-31vf2

⇒0.98×10-19=4.5×10-31vf2

⇒vf2=0.98×10124.5

⇒vf=0.466666×106

=4.6666×105 m s-1=143×105 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two Zener diodes (A and B) having breakdown voltages of 6 V and  4 V respectively, are connected as shown in the circuit below. The output voltage V0 variation with input voltage linearly increasing with time, is given by (Vinput = 0V at t = 0)
question: Two Zener diodes (A and B) having breakdown voltages of 6 V and  4 V respectively, are connected as shown in the circuit below. The output voltage V0 variation with input voltage linearly increasing with time, is given by (Vinput = 0V at t = 0)
solution:
At time t=0, voltage across the first zener diode is increases linearly till it reaches it's breakdown voltage of 4 V, after that voltage increases linearly till it reaches the breakdown voltage of second zener diode,

As soon as voltage across the zener diode reaches the breakdown voltage of 6 V of second zener diode, volatge becomes constant and doesn't change with time.

q: In a given circuit diagram, a 5 V zener diode along with a series resistance is connected across a 50 V power supply. The minimum value of the resistance required, if the maximum zener current is 90 mA will be          Ω.
question: In a given circuit diagram, a 5 V zener diode along with a series resistance is connected across a 50 V power supply. The minimum value of the resistance required, if the maximum zener current is 90 mA will be          Ω.
solution:
Voltage across, RL=5 V

⇒i2=5RL

Also, voltage across R=50-5=45 volt

v=iR⇒R=vi=45ii+i2

R=4590mA+5RL

Current in zener diode is maximum when RL→∞
i2→0 and ii=i

So R=4590 mA=500 Ω

q:
If each diode has a forward bias resistance of 25Ω  in the below circuit,

Which of the following options is correct?
question:
If each diode has a forward bias resistance of 25Ω  in the below circuit,

Which of the following options is correct?
solution:
As the diode D2 is connected in reverse order, there is no current through it.

The effective resistances of the circuit can, then, be calculated as follows:

Reff=25+25+125225+125+25+125 Ω= 100 Ω

Each current through the diodes can be calculated as follows:

I1=5 V100 Ω= 0.05 A

As the diode D2 is non-conducting, the current I1 will be divided equally among the other two diodes D1 and D3.

Hence, the currents in the diodes can be written as

I2=0.05 A2= 0.025 AI4=0.025 A

Thus,

I1I2=0.05 A0.025 A= 2

q: In the given circuit the input voltage Vin  is shown in figure. The cut-in voltage of p-n junction diode (D1 or D2) is 0.6 V. Which of the following output voltage V0 waveform across the diode is correct?
question: In the given circuit the input voltage Vin  is shown in figure. The cut-in voltage of p-n junction diode (D1 or D2) is 0.6 V. Which of the following output voltage V0 waveform across the diode is correct?
solution:
During the positive half cycle of the input waveform, diode D1 is forward biased and diode D2 is reverse biased. Here 0.6 V appears across the output as the diode is conducting. During negative half cycle the diode D1 is reverse biased and D2 is forward biased, so, -0.6 V appears across the output as the diode is conducting in negative direction. This circuit can be used as a voltage limiter. This circuit will not allow any voltage above 0.6 Vand below -0.6 V.

Hence, the output waveform is

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: A diode under reverse-biased condition provides very small current which is nearly independent of voltage until a critical limit at which the current increases drastically.

Reason R: Below the critical voltage limit, only majority charge carriers flow which increases drastically above critical voltage.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: A diode under reverse-biased condition provides very small current which is nearly independent of voltage until a critical limit at which the current increases drastically.

Reason R: Below the critical voltage limit, only majority charge carriers flow which increases drastically above critical voltage.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
In a reverse-biased diode, the current is very small and remains almost constant (known as reverse saturation current) until the breakdown voltage is reached. At the breakdown voltage (critical limit), the current increases drastically. Thus, Assertion A is true.

The small current in reverse bias is due to the flow of minority charge carriers, not majority charge carriers. The drastic increase in current at breakdown is due to the generation of a large number of electron-hole pairs (avalanche or Zener breakdown). Thus, Reason R is false.

Answer: A is true but R is false

question:

Assertion A: Photodiodes are preferably operated in reverse bias condition for light intensity measurement.
Reason R: The current in the forward bias is more than the current in the reverse bias for a p-n junction diode.


In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: A is false but R is true
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
solution: In reverse bias condition of photodiode, the change in saturation reverse current is directly proportional to the change in the incident light flux or light intensity, which can be measured accurately. It is not so when photodiode is forward bias.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following gives a reversible operation?
question: Which of the following gives a reversible operation?
solution:
A logic gate gives the reversible operation if we can recover input data from the output.

For example, a NOT gate gives the reversible operation because we always know that the input is the reverse of the output.

For the given logic gate, let the input is X.

So, the output, Y=X+X¯=X¯ (NOT gate)
